overview.description: The Terraform GCP Landing Zone provisions a deterministic, CIS-aligned foundation in Google Cloud. It is structured as a module registry (vpc-network, iam-service-accounts, gce-hardening, security-baselines, audit-sinks) with a GCS remote state backend, Workload Identity Federation for CI, and policy gates running before every apply. The target consumer is a solo operator or small team running labs at single-region scale; the design intentionally excludes multi-region failover and high-scale production concerns.

components.name: modules/vpc-network
components.purpose: Creates the Private-First VPC with Cloud NAT, Cloud Router, VPC Flow Logs, and IAP tunnel firewall rules.
components.slo: N/A (provisioning module, not a runtime service)
components.failure_mode: Terraform apply fails if CIDR overlap is detected; partial apply is safe because networks are created before NAT rules

components.name: modules/iam-service-accounts
components.purpose: Provisions per-workload service accounts with bindings for impersonation from the CI runner.
components.inputs: project_id, env, workloads (list of workload identifiers)
components.failure_mode: Impersonation failure surfaces as 403 on terraform apply; recoverable by fixing the IAM binding — state is not corrupted

components.name: modules/gce-hardening
components.purpose: Compute Engine instance templates with CIS-hardened OS images, OS Login, auditd, and metadata guards.
components.inputs: image_family (string, default rhel-9), shielded_vm (bool, default true)
components.failure_mode: Instance creation failure on image unavailability; managed instance group rolls back to previous template

components.name: modules/security-baselines
components.purpose: Binds organizational policies (domain restrict, public IP disable, VPC peering constraints) and CIS control metadata labels to resources.
components.failure_mode: Policy binding failure blocks apply entirely (fail-closed); ensures compliance cannot be silently disabled

components.name: modules/audit-sinks
components.purpose: Cloud Logging sinks to BigQuery for 400-day retention + Pub/Sub topic for real-time SIEM export.
components.failure_mode: Sink dropped events are logged to _Required sink as backup; no audit data loss

adrs.title: GCS remote state with native object-locking
adrs.context: Terraform state must be consistent, concurrency-safe, and inside the same trust boundary as the infrastructure it describes. A multi-user lab needed a single source of truth without introducing a cross-cloud dependency or additional SaaS.
adrs.decision: Use a GCS bucket as the remote state backend. Enable object versioning. Rely on GCS native locking via the Cloud Storage object lock on the state file. Grant write access exclusively to the impersonated CI service account; deny write to human identities in production.
adrs.consequences: Positive: single trust boundary (GCP IAM), no new SaaS bill, versioned history of every apply. Negative: lose Terraform Cloud's Sentinel policy UI and apply-run UI; compensated via tfsec/Checkov in CI and GitHub Actions log retention. Operational: state bucket itself is bootstrapped once by a privileged human and then managed exclusively by Terraform thereafter.

adrs.title: Workload Identity Federation for CI (no long-lived keys)
adrs.context: The CI runner (GitHub Actions) must authenticate to GCP to perform terraform apply. Long-lived JSON service account keys are the easy option but violate the CIS credential lifetime control and the NIST IA-5 key-management intent. A stolen CI key has unconstrained lifetime.
adrs.decision: Configure Workload Identity Federation: GCP trusts GitHub's OIDC issuer, a Workload Identity Pool Provider validates the GitHub Actions token, and the CI runner impersonates a per-environment service account via short-lived (1h) credentials. No long-lived keys on disk, in git, or in GitHub Secrets.
adrs.consequences: Positive: zero long-lived credential surface, every apply is attributable to a specific GitHub Actions run, revocation is instant (revoke the pool binding). Negative: 90 minutes of one-time setup (pool, provider, IAM binding, repo-scoped policy). Maintenance: GitHub OIDC issuer URL is stable; no ongoing rotation required.

adrs.title: Private-First VPC with IAP TCP tunnelling for admin
adrs.context: CIS GCP 3.1 requires no public SSH exposure. A bastion host adds an attack surface (the bastion itself) and patching burden. The lab needs occasional admin access to compute tier for debugging and one-off maintenance.
adrs.decision: Set auto_create_subnetworks = false. No compute resource gets a public IPv4. Admin access happens via Cloud IAP TCP tunnelling, authenticated by the operator's IAM identity. IAP audit logs record every session.
adrs.consequences: Positive: zero public inbound listeners, no bastion to patch, every admin session is attributable via IAM audit logs. Negative: IAP has a per-byte cost (~$0.50/month observed at lab scale). Operational: operators must have `gcloud` + IAP IAM bindings; this is documented in onboarding.

adrs.title: Module registry over flat root module
adrs.context: Early versions of this project were a single flat main.tf. A one-character typo in a firewall rule forced a re-plan of the entire stack (~90s) and made code review noisy. Blast radius was unbounded.
adrs.decision: Split into the module registry (vpc-network, iam-service-accounts, gce-hardening, security-baselines, audit-sinks). Each module has explicit input/output contracts and its own README. Root module composes modules but owns minimal resources directly.
adrs.consequences: Positive: plan feedback time per change ~8s (from ~90s), blast radius scoped per-module, code review is tractable. Negative: more files to navigate. Mitigated by a top-level README.md that shows the module dependency tree.

adrs.title: tfsec as CI gate, Checkov as advisory
adrs.context: Both tfsec and Checkov scan Terraform for misconfigurations. They disagree on severity classification roughly 15% of the time. Running both as gates produces false-positive blockage; running neither means shipping unchecked.
adrs.decision: tfsec is the hard CI gate — HIGH findings block merge. Checkov runs in advisory mode (soft_fail: true) and posts results as a PR comment for human review. New findings in Checkov that tfsec misses trigger a discussion, not a block.
adrs.consequences: Positive: deterministic CI outcome, second opinion preserved. Negative: the team must actually read Checkov advisories (process risk). Operational: quarterly review of accepted Checkov findings to catch drift in the project's risk posture.

config_walkthrough.why: Provider versions pinned with ~> 5.0 allow security-patch minors but block major upgrades without explicit migration. The impersonate_service_account directive fires at backend-init time — any laptop run fails unless the operator has the impersonation IAM binding.

config_walkthrough.code:
---
severity_overrides:
  google-compute-no-public-ingress: CRITICAL
  google-compute-disk-encryption-customer-key: HIGH
  google-storage-bucket-encryption-customer-key: HIGH

exclude:
  - google-gke-use-cluster-labels  # lab uses compute, not GKE

minimum_severity: HIGH
config_walkthrough.why: Severity overrides bind tfsec's classification to this project's risk model. google-compute-no-public-ingress becomes CRITICAL because a single leaked public ingress rule defeats the entire Private-First architecture — it must hard-block merges. Exclusions are documented per rule with the reason.

The attribute_condition is the load-bearing line. Without it, any GitHub Actions run anywhere in the world can federate to this pool and request impersonation. With it, only actions from this specific repository can. This is the OIDC equivalent of a firewall rule — get it wrong and the entire security model collapses.

deploy: # 1. Authenticate (developer laptop — one-time IAP + iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator grant)
# 2. Open a PR with the change. CI runs: tfsec gate → terraform plan → human approval → terraform apply
# 3. Merge PR; CI auto-applies on main branch
# Emergency local apply (requires break-glass IAM role):
rollback: # Terraform rollback = revert the commit and re-apply the previous state.
# State is in GCS; every apply is a new state version.
# Option A: revert in git, let CI apply
git push  # CI picks up and applies the reverted state
# Option B: emergency state rollback to a specific GCS version
# Then manually restore via terraform state push (break-glass only, logs an incident)

steps: Create new directory envs/staging-2/ cloning envs/staging/ structure
Update terraform.tfvars with staging-2-specific CIDRs, project_id
Update GCS backend prefix to envs/staging-2/v1
Add GitHub Actions workflow dispatch target for staging-2
Bind Workload Identity pool to new environment SA
Open PR; CI plans against fresh empty project; apply creates env

common_ops.task: Rotate an IAM service account (quarterly hygiene)
steps: Modify modules/iam-service-accounts/main.tf to change SA name suffix (e.g. -v2)
Apply — creates new SA alongside old
Migrate workload references to new SA
Apply again to remove old SA
Verify Cloud Audit logs show impersonation events against new SA only

incidents.scenario: Terraform state lock acquired but CI job failed mid-apply
incidents.response: GCS native locking auto-releases after ~5 minutes of orphan. If urgent: gsutil stat gs://.../default.tfstate.tflock → confirm it's stale (timestamp > 15min) → gsutil rm gs://.../default.tfstate.tflock. Then re-run the apply job. Check state integrity with terraform plan — expect no-diff if the prior apply had completed its writes.

incidents.scenario: Out-of-band drift detected (resource modified via GCP Console)
incidents.response: Do NOT re-run terraform apply blindly. Run terraform plan and inspect the diff. If the console change was accidental → confirm the terraform state is correct and apply reconciles. If the console change was intentional but not yet in terraform → capture the change as code in a PR, then apply. Log the incident in the Operations Log (docs/ops-log.md).

incidents.scenario: Workload Identity Federation fails: Unable to acquire impersonated credentials
incidents.response: Check (1) attribute_condition in workload-identity-federation.tf matches the current repo identity, (2) the GitHub Actions workflow has permissions: id-token: write, (3) the impersonator_sa has roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator granted to the principal. If all three pass, the GCP OIDC trust config may be stale — recreate the pool provider via terraform apply -target.

threat_model.threat: Compromised GitHub Actions runner impersonates the apply SA and provisions a malicious resource
threat_model.mitigation: attribute_condition scopes the OIDC trust to a single repo. Branch protection on main prevents unauthorized pushes. The apply SA has the minimum set of roles needed; destructive capabilities are split across multiple SAs so a single compromise cannot wipe the landing zone.

threat_model.threat: Terraform state file corruption (partial write during failure)
threat_model.mitigation: GCS object versioning retains every state version. Emergency rollback procedure in runbook. Integrity verified via terraform plan expecting no-diff after recovery.

threat_model.threat: Leaked tfstate reveals sensitive configuration (e.g., IP ranges, resource names)
threat_model.mitigation: State bucket is IAM-restricted — only the impersonated apply SA and a break-glass human admin group have get/set. State bucket is in the same project as the landing zone, not a separate admin project. Bucket has Uniform Bucket Access and public access prevention enforced.

threat_model.threat: Out-of-band console change bypasses tfsec gate
threat_model.mitigation: Nightly drift-detection CI runs terraform plan on a schedule; non-zero diff posts a PR-ready change and alerts. Organization policies enforce the most critical constraints (no public IPs, domain-restricted SAs) at GCP's admission layer — not just in Terraform.

threat_model.threat: Insider modifies CI pipeline to disable tfsec gate
threat_model.mitigation: CODEOWNERS on .github/workflows/ requires review from security-designated approver. Branch protection on main requires 1+ approval for any workflow change. GitHub audit log tracks workflow modifications.

unresolved_risks: Single-region failure domain — a regional GCP outage takes the entire landing zone down. Acceptable at lab scale; production would require multi-region replication of state and workloads.
No dual-admin approval for break-glass state operations — a single break-glass human has unilateral state-push capability. Production would require a two-person rule via approver IAM role.
Organization policies are enforced at the project level, not the folder level — a future migration to a multi-project folder hierarchy would need org policy reshaping.

metrics.name: terraform_apply_duration_seconds
metrics.source: GitHub Actions workflow timing, scraped into Prometheus via pushgateway

metrics.name: drift_detected_total
metrics.source: Nightly terraform plan cron job — emits metric if terraform show -json plan.tfplan | jq .resource_changes yields non-zero length

metrics.name: sa_key_created_total
metrics.threshold: = 0 (any SA key creation is an alert)
metrics.source: GCP Cloud Audit Log → Pub/Sub sink → alerting policy

metrics.name: iap_tunnel_sessions_total
metrics.threshold: spike > 3σ of weekly baseline triggers review
metrics.source: Cloud IAP audit logs → BigQuery scheduled query

alerts.name: terraform_apply_failed
alerts.condition: apply job exits non-zero on main branch
alerts.action: Page on-call via PagerDuty; open auto-incident; block further merges until resolved

alerts.name: drift_detected
alerts.action: File a PR with the console-induced drift; assign to last-modified owner; notify #infra-drift Slack

alerts.name: sa_key_created
alerts.condition: any IAM service account key creation event
alerts.action: Page immediately. SA key creation violates the no-long-lived-credentials policy; investigate source.

dashboards: Terraform Apply Health — GitHub Actions run duration, failure rate by module, average plan size
Landing Zone Compliance — live CIS pass/fail count, tfsec finding trend over time, accepted-tradeoff list
IAM + IAP — impersonation events per SA, IAP tunnel sessions per operator, anomaly detection on unusual session times

testing: Module tests live in modules/*/test/ using Terratest (Go). Each module test: (a) terraform init+apply against an ephemeral GCP project created by the test harness, (b) assertions on the output (e.g., verifying google_compute_network.primary.auto_create_subnetworks = false), (c) terraform destroy and project teardown. Target coverage is at least one assert per architectural invariant per module. Tests run on every PR via GitHub Actions using the same Workload Identity Federation as production — giving CI parity with apply. Integration tests additionally hit the live CIS scanner (tfsec) against the applied state. There are currently no chaos or load tests — lab scale does not justify them.
limitations: Single-region deployment — multi-region would require state replication and duplicated module instances per region, not attempted.

Five CIS GCP Foundations Benchmark v2.0 controls currently fail: 2.14 (log sink to BigQuery encryption), 3.6 (CloudSQL public IP), 3.10 (DNSSEC for public zones — no public zones), 4.1 (legacy networks — none present), 5.11 (BigQuery column-level security for audit dataset — deferred, low traffic). All documented in reports/cis-scan-2026-04-15.txt with accept/defer rationale.
No chaos testing — terraform destroy is not regularly exercised outside test harness runs. A catastrophic bug in a destroy path would only surface in emergency.
GitHub Actions is the only supported CI — migration to GitLab CI / CircleCI would require re-engineering the Workload Identity Federation provider.
RHEL 9 is the only supported compute OS in gce-hardening — Ubuntu / Debian variants would need parallel hardening modules.

references.label: CIS Google Cloud Foundations Benchmark v2.0
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — Security and Privacy Controls
